name: Lutetium
name_origin: From Lutetia, the Latin name for Paris, the home city of its discoverer Georges Urbain.

discovery.discoverer: Georges Urbain; independently by Carl Auer von Welsbach and Charles James

occurrence.abundance_summary: The least abundant of the lanthanides aside from promethium (~0.8 ppm in crust); recovered chiefly as a minor component of xenotime, monazite, and ion-adsorption clays.

compounds.name: Lutetium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: White solid used as a precursor for lutetium-doped scintillators and high-refractive-index optical glass.

compounds.name: Lutetium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: White hygroscopic salt; precursor for lutetium metal and for medical radiopharmaceuticals based on Lu-177.

compounds.name: Lutetium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Used as feedstock for high-purity lutetium metal and in fluoride scintillator research.

compounds.name: Lutetium oxyorthosilicate (LSO)
compounds.summary: Cerium-doped LSO is a high-density, fast scintillator widely used in medical PET detectors.

uses: Cerium-doped LSO and LYSO scintillators in positron emission tomography (PET) scanners
Lu-177 chelate radiopharmaceuticals for targeted therapy of neuroendocrine and prostate cancers
Catalysts in petroleum cracking, alkylation, and polymerisation
High-refractive-index lutetium oxide additive in specialty optical glass
Bone-age determination via Lu-176/Hf-176 dating in geochronology
Dopant in solid-state lasers and X-ray phosphors
reactivity_summary: Silvery-white, dense, relatively hard rare-earth metal; the most stable lanthanide in air. Reacts slowly with water and dissolves in dilute acids; powdered metal can ignite at elevated temperature.
isotopes_summary: Lutetium has two naturally occurring isotopes: stable Lu-175 (~97.4%) and primordial radioactive Lu-176 (half-life 3.78x10^10 years), used in Lu-Hf geochronology. Synthetic Lu-177 (6.65 days) is widely used in targeted radiotherapy.
